content:
14 min read
Just now

--

How developers, technical leaders, and teams can preserve understanding as coding agents accelerate implementation.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Imagine a pull request for an external provider integration.
The code looks clean. The tests pass. The coding agent gives you a polished summary of what changed. Nothing in the diff looks obviously dangerous.
Then a reviewer asks a simple question:
What happens if the provider times out after the database write succeeds?
The room goes quiet.
The developer reopens the agent conversation. Another engineer searches through the tests. Someone asks the agent to trace the flow again.
The team has evidence that selected paths work. It has documentation describing the change. What it does not have is a shared mental model of the behaviour.
That gap is cognitive debt.
I have seen a version of this problem while working with AI-assisted development across multiple components. The code was not the only source of complexity. Context itself had to be separated into current state, authoritative sources, decisions, historical material, superseded information, and assumptions that had not yet been verified.
The lesson was not simply “give the agent more context.”
Humans and agents both need to know what is current, what is authoritative, what is historical, and what remains uncertain.
Otherwise, implementation can move forward while understanding falls behind.
Cognitive debt is not another name for bad code
Margaret-Anne Storey gives us a useful distinction in her Triple Debt Model.
Technical debt lives in the code
It includes familiar problems:

• tight coupling

• duplicated logic

• fragile abstractions

• missing tests

• poor structure

• shortcuts that make future change harder.
Cognitive debt lives in people and teams
It appears when:

• engineers cannot explain why the system works this way

• simple changes feel dangerous

• every debugging session starts with broad rediscovery

• one person holds most of the system theory

• reviews depend on generated summaries

• the codebase feels like a black box even when it looks clean.
Intent debt lives in externalized knowledge
It grows when:

• rationale is missing

• goals and constraints were never recorded

• specifications drift away from implementation

• decisions remain inside private conversations

• historical documents are mistaken for current truth.
These debts interact, but they are not interchangeable.
Refactoring can reduce technical debt. Documentation can reduce intent debt. Neither automatically repays cognitive debt.
You can clean the code without rebuilding the team’s understanding. You can generate more documentation without knowing whether anyone can use it to reason about the system.
Cognitive debt is repaid when people rebuild a working theory of what the software does, why it behaves that way, and how it can change safely.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Technical debt lives in code, cognitive debt lives in people, and intent debt lives in the knowledge that connects decisions to implementation.A program is more than its source code
Peter Naur made this argument in 1985 in Programming as Theory Building.
His view was that programming is not mainly the production of source code and supporting documents. It is the activity through which programmers build a theory of the problem and its solution.
An engineer who possesses that theory can do more than read the code. They can:

• explain how the system maps to the real-world problem

• justify important design choices

• answer questions about unexpected behaviour

• recognize when a proposed change damages the system’s structure

• apply the design to a new situation.
Naur described a compiler project where one team received the source code, extensive documentation, and advice from the original team. Even with those materials, the receiving team proposed extensions that weakened the compiler’s existing structure. The original designers could immediately see better solutions because they still held the deeper theory.
The documents contained information.
The original team held an understanding.
That distinction matters even more when an agent can generate a complete implementation before a human has formed the corresponding mental model.
The program may work. The tests may pass. The explanation may sound convincing.
But can the responsible engineer predict what happens when the environment changes?
Can they explain why one recovery path exists and another does not?
Can they safely make the next decision without asking the agent to reconstruct the system from scratch?
That is the real comprehension test.
Passing tests is not the same as understanding
I care deeply about verification. I do not trust an agent’s final summary as proof that its implementation works.
But verification and comprehension answer different questions.
Verification asks: What evidence shows that this behaviour worked?
Comprehension asks: Did my mental model predict that behaviour, and can I reason about what happens outside the tested path?
A test suite can prove that selected scenarios behave as expected. It cannot automatically prove that the team understands why those scenarios matter, which assumptions the tests encode, or which important behaviour nobody thought to test.
Documentation has a similar boundary.
Good documentation preserves rationale, constraints, terminology, operational procedures, and navigation. Bad documentation merely translates code into prose and creates another source of truth that can become stale.
Even excellent documentation does not install a mental model into someone’s head.
Research is starting to show why the way we use AI matters. In a randomized study by Anthropic, 52 mostly junior developers learned a new Python library. The AI-assisted group averaged 50 percent on an immediate mastery quiz, compared with 67 percent for the hand-coding group.
That result needs boundaries.
The sample was small. The participants were learning the Trio library. The quiz happened shortly after the coding task. The study does not show that AI permanently makes developers worse or that every professional coding task produces the same effect.
The more useful finding is the qualitative one.
Participants who relied heavily on AI delegation or AI-led debugging tended to score lower, while higher-scoring patterns were associated with conceptual questions, requests for explanations, and generation followed by deliberate comprehension work. Anthropic explicitly notes that this qualitative analysis does not establish causality.
AI was not automatically the problem.
The risk grew when assistance replaced active reasoning.
This article focuses on what happens after an implementation exists. For the companion problems around hidden assumptions and unsupported completion claims, see Your Coding Agent Is Guessing. You Just Can’t See Where. And Your Coding Agent Will Always Tell You It’s Safe. Don’t Believe It.
A separate 2026 preprint on undergraduate software-engineering projects offers a related qualitative signal. Based on reflective diaries, it identified four comprehension-debt accumulation patterns: AI-as-black-box code acceptance, context-mismatch debt, dependency-induced atrophy, and verification-bypass. It identified one mitigating pattern: using AI as a comprehension scaffold.
Again, this was an undergraduate setting based on reflective diaries, not a production-team experiment. But the direction is useful:
The same tool can substitute for understanding or help build it. The workflow decides which role it plays.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Clean code, passing tests, and polished explanations can coexist with a team that cannot predict or safely extend the system.Three ways AI can help rebuild understanding
A recent talk by Geoffrey Litt, Understanding is the New Bottleneck, changed how I think about this problem.
The talk does not argue that we should stop using coding agents. It asks how we can use the same agents to help humans keep up with the code they produce.
Litt presents three practical techniques.
1. Explanations that teach, not summarize
A raw diff is organized by files. A good explanation is organized through learning.
Litt’s explainer starts with the existing system, then builds intuition before introducing implementation details. It teaches the concepts needed to understand the change. Where it helps, it also uses selective interactive figures to make behaviour easier to grasp. Only after that does it walk through the code as a literate diff in explanatory order.
This is different from asking:
Summarize this pull request.A better request is:
Teach me this change.Start with how the existing system works.
Explain the goal and the important concepts before showing code.
Present the code in the order that makes the behaviour easiest to understand.
Identify assumptions, failure paths, and questions I should be able to answer afterward.
Ground every explanation in source code, tests, or runtime evidence.
A change summary tells you what files changed.
A learning-oriented explanation helps you build the mental model needed to reason about those changes.
2. Microworlds that expose hidden behaviour
A microworld is a small, temporary environment where you can manipulate one part of a system and observe what happens.
For backend work, an agent could build a temporary:

• retry timeline

• transaction-state viewer

• queue-processing visualizer

• protocol simulator

• failure-injection harness

• request-trace explorer
This produces one of my favourite ideas from the talk:
Agents can write temporary code that helps humans understand permanent code.
The temporary tool does not need production polishing. It needs to make an important behaviour visible.
Microworlds can also become decorative slop. An interactive diagram that does not improve prediction or reasoning is just another artifact to maintain. Use them where state, time, concurrency, geometry, protocols, or failure paths are difficult to understand statically.
3. Shared spaces that create collective understanding
Private conversations with an agent create a private understanding.
That is useful for one person. It does not automatically help the team.
Litt argues for shared spaces where multiple people and agents can see and discuss plans, explanations, terms, decisions, and questions.
The tool does not have to be Notion. It could be:

• a pull request

• an ADR

• repository documentation

• a shared agent session

• a domain glossary

• a runbook

• a diagram

• a collaborative planning document
The important part is that the reasoning becomes visible, challengeable, and durable.
The Comprehension Loop
The video techniques are useful, but I wanted an engineering workflow that connects them to prediction, runtime evidence, ownership, and team knowledge.
This is the Comprehension Loop:
-> Explain
-> Predict
-> Explore
-> Verify
-> Explain Back
-> SharePress enter or click to view image in full size

Important AI-assisted changes should produce explanation, prediction, exploration, evidence, human ownership, and shared understanding.Let us walk through the provider-integration example.
1. Explain
Start with the existing system, not the diff.
The engineer should understand:

• where the request enters

• when local state changes

• when the provider is called

• what retry mechanism exists

• which identifiers support idempotency

• what the caller sees

• which component owns recovery
The agent can generate the first explanation, but it must cite the relevant code, tests, and decisions. A confident explanation can still be wrong.
2. Predict
Before running the system, make the mental model visible.
Ask the responsible engineer to predict:

• What happens when the provider succeeds?

• What happens when it times out before the local write?

• What happens when it times out after the local write?

• What pre
